title: DSA in Kotlin
description: Algorithm problems in Kotlin — the patterns that come up in Android interviews at top firms
lesson:
Data Structures & Algorithms for Android Interviews

FAANG Android interviews include 2-3 coding rounds with standard DSA problems, but you write them in Kotlin. Key advantages of Kotlin for DSA:

  • Collection functions: filter, map, groupBy, sortedBy, sumOf, windowed
  • Destructuring: val (key, value) = pair
  • String templates: "${result}"
  • Extension functions: write clean helpers
  • Ranges: for (i in 0 until n), (n downTo 0)

Must-Know Patterns for Android Interviews

Every coding interview draws from a small set of recurring patterns. Recognizing which pattern applies is half the battle.

1. Two Pointers

Use when the input is sorted or you need to compare elements from both ends. Classic problems: palindrome check, container with most water, remove duplicates in-place.

// Two pointers — find pair that sums to target in SORTED array fun twoSumSorted(nums: IntArray, target: Int): Pair<Int, Int> { var left = 0 var right = nums.lastIndex while (left < right) { val sum = nums[left] + nums[right] when { sum == target -> return left to right sum < target -> left++ else -> right-- } } throw IllegalArgumentException("No solution") } // Time: O(n), Space: O(1) — no extra data structure needed

2. Sliding Window

Use for subarray/substring problems with a contiguous constraint. Two flavors: fixed-size window and variable-size window.

// Fixed window — max sum of any k consecutive elements fun maxSumWindow(nums: IntArray, k: Int): Int { var windowSum = nums.take(k).sum() var maxSum = windowSum for (i in k until nums.size) { windowSum += nums[i] - nums[i - k] // slide: add right, remove left maxSum = maxOf(maxSum, windowSum) } return maxSum } // Variable window — longest substring without repeating chars fun lengthOfLongestSubstring(s: String): Int { val seen = mutableMapOf<Char, Int>() // char -> last index var left = 0 var maxLen = 0 for ((right, c) in s.withIndex()) { seen[c]?.let { left = maxOf(left, it + 1) } seen[c] = right maxLen = maxOf(maxLen, right - left + 1) } return maxLen }

3. HashMap / Frequency Counting

The workhorse of interview problems. Use for lookups, counting, grouping, and two-sum style problems.

// Two Sum — the most-asked interview question ever fun twoSum(nums: IntArray, target: Int): IntArray { val seen = mutableMapOf<Int, Int>() // value -> index for ((i, num) in nums.withIndex()) { val complement = target - num seen[complement]?.let { return intArrayOf(it, i) } seen[num] = i } throw IllegalArgumentException("No solution") } // Key insight: for each num, ask "have I seen target-num before?" // Time: O(n), Space: O(n)

4. Stack

Use for matching brackets, monotonic sequences, next greater element, and expression evaluation.

// Valid Parentheses — classic stack problem fun isValid(s: String): Boolean { val stack = ArrayDeque<Char>() val pairs = mapOf(')' to '(', ']' to '[', '}' to '{') for (c in s) { if (c in pairs.values) { stack.addLast(c) } else { if (stack.isEmpty() || stack.removeLast() != pairs[c]) return false } } return stack.isEmpty() }

5. BFS / DFS

BFS for shortest path (unweighted), level-order traversal. DFS for exhaustive search, path finding, connected components.

// Binary Tree Level Order Traversal — BFS with Queue fun levelOrder(root: TreeNode?): List<List<Int>> { if (root == null) return emptyList() val result = mutableListOf<List<Int>>() val queue = ArrayDeque<TreeNode>() queue.addLast(root) while (queue.isNotEmpty()) { val level = mutableListOf<Int>() repeat(queue.size) { // process entire level val node = queue.removeFirst() level.add(node.`val`) node.left?.let { queue.addLast(it) } node.right?.let { queue.addLast(it) } } result.add(level) } return result } // Key: repeat(queue.size) captures the level size BEFORE adding children

6. Binary Search

Use on sorted data or any search space that can be halved. Look for "find minimum/maximum that satisfies condition."

// Standard binary search in Kotlin fun binarySearch(nums: IntArray, target: Int): Int { var lo = 0 var hi = nums.lastIndex while (lo <= hi) { val mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 // prevents overflow when { nums[mid] == target -> return mid nums[mid] < target -> lo = mid + 1 else -> hi = mid - 1 } } return -1 // not found } // Kotlin stdlib: nums.asList().binarySearch(target)

7. Dynamic Programming

Break problem into overlapping subproblems. Two approaches: top-down (memoization) and bottom-up (tabulation).

// Climbing stairs — bottom-up DP fun climbStairs(n: Int): Int { if (n <= 2) return n var prev2 = 1 // dp[i-2] var prev1 = 2 // dp[i-1] for (i in 3..n) { val curr = prev1 + prev2 prev2 = prev1 prev1 = curr } return prev1 } // Coin change — classic DP with array fun coinChange(coins: IntArray, amount: Int): Int { val dp = IntArray(amount + 1) { amount + 1 } dp[0] = 0 for (i in 1..amount) { for (coin in coins) { if (coin <= i) dp[i] = minOf(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1) } } return if (dp[amount] > amount) -1 else dp[amount] }

8. Backtracking

Generate all combinations, permutations, or valid configurations. Prune invalid paths early.

// Generate all subsets (power set) fun subsets(nums: IntArray): List<List<Int>> { val result = mutableListOf<List<Int>>() fun backtrack(start: Int, current: MutableList<Int>) { result.add(current.toList()) // copy snapshot for (i in start until nums.size) { current.add(nums[i]) // choose backtrack(i + 1, current) // explore current.removeLast() // un-choose (backtrack) } } backtrack(0, mutableListOf()) return result }

Common Interview Problems with Kotlin Solutions

Merge Intervals

A frequent interview question. Sort by start, then merge overlapping intervals.

fun merge(intervals: Array<IntArray>): Array<IntArray> { if (intervals.isEmpty()) return emptyArray() intervals.sortBy { it[0] } // sort by start time val merged = mutableListOf(intervals[0]) for (interval in intervals) { val last = merged.last() if (interval[0] <= last[1]) { // Overlapping — extend the end last[1] = maxOf(last[1], interval[1]) } else { merged.add(interval) } } return merged.toTypedArray() } // Time: O(n log n) for sort, Space: O(n)

LRU Cache (LinkedHashMap)

Directly relevant to image caching in Android. LinkedHashMap with access order gives O(1) get and put.

class LRUCache(private val capacity: Int) { // accessOrder=true: entries ordered by last access, not insertion private val cache = object : LinkedHashMap<Int, Int>( capacity, 0.75f, true ) { override fun removeEldestEntry( eldest: MutableMap.MutableEntry<Int, Int>? ) = size > capacity } fun get(key: Int): Int = cache.getOrDefault(key, -1) fun put(key: Int, value: Int) { cache[key] = value } } // This is exactly how Android's LruCache works internally

Kotlin-Specific Idioms for DSA

Kotlin's stdlib gives you expressive tools that make DSA code shorter and less error-prone.

// Destructuring in loops for ((index, value) in nums.withIndex()) { ... } for ((key, value) in map) { ... } val (first, second) = pair // Sequences for lazy evaluation (large datasets) val result = (1..1_000_000).asSequence() .filter { it % 2 == 0 } .map { it * it } .take(10) // stops early — only processes 20 elements .toList() // buildList — build a list with a mutable builder val evens = buildList { for (i in 0..20) if (i % 2 == 0) add(i) } // groupBy — group elements by a key val byLength = words.groupBy { it.length } // {3=["the","cat"], 5=["hello","world"]} // partition — split into two lists by predicate val (passed, failed) = students.partition { it.score >= 60 } // zip — combine two lists pairwise val names = listOf("Alice", "Bob") val scores = listOf(95, 87) val pairs = names.zip(scores) // [(Alice,95), (Bob,87)] // Frequency counting — idiomatic one-liner val freq = str.groupingBy { it }.eachCount() // ArrayDeque as stack AND queue val stack = ArrayDeque<Int>() stack.addLast(1) // push stack.removeLast() // pop val queue = ArrayDeque<Int>() queue.addLast(1) // enqueue queue.removeFirst() // dequeue

String Manipulation in Kotlin

String problems are common in interviews. Kotlin makes them much cleaner than Java.

// StringBuilder for efficient concatenation val sb = StringBuilder() for (c in str) { if (c.isLetterOrDigit()) sb.append(c.lowercaseChar()) } val cleaned = sb.toString() // Or use buildString (Kotlin idiom) val cleaned = buildString { for (c in str) if (c.isLetterOrDigit()) append(c.lowercaseChar()) } // Regex patterns val digits = "abc123def456".replace(Regex("[^0-9]"), "") // "123456" val words = "hello world".split(Regex("\\s+")) // [hello, world] val isEmail = str.matches(Regex("^[\\w.]+@[\\w.]+$")) // Common patterns str.reversed() // reverse a string str.toCharArray().also { it.sort() }.concatToString() // sort chars str.windowed(3) // sliding window of size 3 str.zipWithNext() // pairs of adjacent chars str.count { it == 'a' } // count occurrences

Kotlin Collections Performance

Choosing the right collection can make or break your solution's time complexity.

Collectionget/setadd/removecontainsNotes
ArrayListO(1)O(n) worstO(n)Default mutableListOf(). Best for random access.
LinkedListO(n)O(1) at endsO(n)Rarely better than ArrayList in practice due to cache misses.
HashMapO(1) avgO(1) avgO(1) avgDefault mutableMapOf(). Use for almost all map needs.
TreeMapO(log n)O(log n)O(log n)Sorted keys. Use when you need floor/ceiling/range queries.
HashSet—O(1) avgO(1) avgDefault mutableSetOf(). Fast membership testing.
TreeSet—O(log n)O(log n)Sorted set. Useful for ordered iteration.
ArrayDequeO(1) endsO(1) endsO(n)Use as both stack and queue. Faster than LinkedList.
PriorityQueueO(1) peekO(log n)O(n)Min-heap by default. Use for top-K, merge-K-sorted.
Interview tip: When you say "I'll use a HashMap for O(1) lookup" — be ready to explain what happens on hash collisions (chaining, O(n) worst case) and when a TreeMap (O(log n) guaranteed) might be better.

Time & Space Complexity Cheat Sheet

Algorithm / PatternTimeSpaceWhen to Use
Two PointersO(n)O(1)Sorted array, palindromes, pair finding
Sliding WindowO(n)O(k)Contiguous subarray/substring, fixed or variable window
HashMap LookupO(n)O(n)Two Sum, frequency counting, anagram grouping
Binary SearchO(log n)O(1)Sorted data, search space reduction, rotated arrays
BFS (graph/tree)O(V + E)O(V)Shortest path (unweighted), level-order traversal
DFS (graph/tree)O(V + E)O(V)Path finding, cycle detection, connected components
SortingO(n log n)O(n)Merge intervals, meeting rooms, custom ordering
Dynamic ProgrammingvariesO(n) typicalOptimal substructure + overlapping subproblems
BacktrackingO(2^n) / O(n!)O(n)Permutations, combinations, constraint satisfaction
Heap / Priority QueueO(n log k)O(k)Top K elements, merge K sorted lists, median stream
Common mistake: Don't confuse O(n) average with O(n) worst case. HashMap is O(1) average but O(n) worst case. In interviews, state which you mean. Interviewers notice.

Pattern Recognition Guide

The fastest way to solve interview problems is recognizing which pattern applies:

Problem says... → Try this pattern ────────────────────────────────────────────────── "sorted array" → Two Pointers or Binary Search "subarray / substring" → Sliding Window "pairs that sum to X" → HashMap (Two Sum pattern) "all combinations/permutations" → Backtracking "shortest path" → BFS "connected components" → DFS or Union-Find "top K" or "Kth largest" → Heap (PriorityQueue) "optimal / minimum / maximum" → DP (check overlapping subproblems) "intervals" → Sort by start, then merge/sweep "tree level by level" → BFS with queue "valid brackets/nesting" → Stack "next greater/smaller element" → Monotonic Stack

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.description: Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of two numbers that add up to target. Each input has exactly one solution.

checks.message: Use a HashMap for O(n) lookup

questions.explanation: For each number, calculate its complement (target - num). Check if we've already seen the complement. If yes, return both indices. If no, store current number and index. This is O(n) time and O(n) space. The Kotlin idiom seen[complement]?.let { } is cleaner than null-checking.
hints: For each number, what other number would you need to reach the target?
A HashMap lets you check "have I seen this number before?" in O(1)

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.description: Given a string containing just (){}[], determine if the input string is valid. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
questions.explanation: Push opening brackets onto the stack. For closing brackets, check if the top of the stack matches. If not, invalid. At the end, the stack should be empty (all brackets matched). ArrayDeque is Kotlin's preferred stack/deque implementation. Time: O(n), Space: O(n).
hints: When you see a closing bracket, what should be on top of the stack?
Don't forget to check if the stack is empty at the end

questions.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's)
questions.description: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum and return the sum.
questions.explanation: Kadane's Algorithm: at each position, decide whether to extend the current subarray or start fresh. If currentSum + nums[i] is less than nums[i] alone, start a new subarray. Track the global maximum. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). This is a classic DP problem where the state is "max subarray ending at index i".
hints: At each element, you have two choices: extend the current subarray, or start a new one
Which choice gives a larger sum?

questions.title: LRU Cache
questions.description: Design a Least Recently Used cache with get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) time. This is directly relevant to image caching in Android.
questions.explanation: LinkedHashMap with accessOrder = true (3rd constructor param) maintains entries in access order — most recently accessed at the end. Override removeEldestEntry to auto-evict when over capacity. This gives O(1) get and put. In Android, this is exactly how LruCache (used by image loaders) works internally. Understanding this data structure is critical for mobile performance.
hints: LinkedHashMap can maintain insertion order OR access order
Java/Kotlin has a built-in data structure perfect for LRU

questions.title: BFS — Shortest Path in Grid
questions.description: Given a 2D grid where 0 is open and 1 is blocked, find the shortest path from top-left to bottom-right. Return the path length or -1 if impossible.
questions.explanation: BFS guarantees shortest path in an unweighted graph/grid. Use a queue (FIFO): explore all cells at distance 1, then distance 2, etc. Mark cells as visited when enqueued (not when dequeued) to prevent revisits. The dirs array trick encodes 4 directions compactly. Time: O(rows * cols), Space: O(rows * cols). This pattern appears in many mobile-relevant problems (map navigation, game pathfinding).
hints: BFS always finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs
Use a queue, not a stack (that would be DFS)

questions.title: Time Complexity
questions.description: What is the time complexity of this Kotlin code?
options: O(n) — single pass with O(1) HashSet lookups
questions.explanation: The loop runs n times. Each in seen (contains) and add operation on a HashSet is O(1) average case. Total: O(n) time, O(n) space. This is the standard pattern for detecting duplicates efficiently. Compare with the naive O(n^2) approach of checking every pair.
hints: What is the time complexity of HashSet.contains()?
How many times does the loop run?
